items.excerpt: StatTC has arrived with the newest 1500m rankings
items.author: Tate Sheehy
items.title: Men's 1500m Rankings

items.excerpt: The strange case of Jonah Koech and track's transparency problem
items.body:
Picture this: The NBA season is underway – well underway – and Stephen Curry has yet to play. That would be unfortunate, but nothing out of the ordinary. Injuries happen; it’s part of the game. Now imagine we have no idea why Curry isn’t playing. As a matter of fact, we have no idea *where he even is*. His agent doesn’t know where he is, and his coach and team don’t know what he’s doing.

	This isn’t just a distant fantasy. Actually, it’s the reality for Jonah Koech, the reigning US champion in the 1500m. Koech hasn’t raced since finishing 13th at the World Championships in the 1500m in September. [According to LetsRun](https://www.letsrun.com/news/2026/06/where-are-us-stars-jonah-koech-josh-hoey-athing-mu-nikolayev/), Koech missed the indoor season after suffering a knee injury over the winter, but his current status has become increasingly unclear. He is no longer represented by agent Peter Wolters or part of Under Armour Mission Run Baltimore, though he remains sponsored by Under Armour, and neither Wolters nor former coach Tom Brumlik knows his current whereabouts. Think about that for a second. Koech isn’t a fringe professional or an athlete quietly on their way to retirement. This is the reigning U.S. champion in one of track's premier events, and with the outdoor season in full swing, and nobody seems to know where he is or whether he'll race again.

	This article isn’t designed to cast doubt on Koech’s prior performances or imply that he is serving a doping suspension. For what it’s worth, there isn’t actually any evidence of any of that. Any discussion of the matter would be purely speculative. Rather, this should serve as a reflection on just how unusual this situation is. Maybe there's a perfectly reasonable explanation. Maybe Koech simply wants privacy while dealing with matters away from the track. If that's the case, that's his right. But elite athletes, especially reigning national champions, don't typically vanish in the middle of a season without so much as a public update (even more mysterious, perhaps, is the situation of Athing Mu, but that’s a case for another time).

	This, and other situations all too familiar to track fans, beg the question: what onus do athletes have to their fans? When an athlete reaches the pinnacle of their sport, they also become a public figure. While no one is *entitled* to the intimate details of an athlete's personal life, it's fair to ask whether there is some responsibility to communicate when they disappear indefinitely from competition. In this instance, and most instances, I believe a simple explanation would go a long way for Koech. He could quell discussions of a doping suspension and give fans, like me, an explanation for his absence so we know when to expect him back on the circuit.

	To revisit the NBA analogy, what makes it so difficult to keep track of our favorite track athletes, and so much easier to keep tabs on our favorite NBA, NFL, or soccer players? Team sports are built around franchises with media departments, injury reports, beat reporters, league-wide news cycles, and contractual obligations that keep fans informed. If Stephen Curry suffers a sprained ankle, the Warriors will announce it. If he disappears from the team, Stephen A. Smith and Nick Wright are speculating on it within hours, and Shams Charania is on the case.

Track operates almost entirely differently. Athletes are effectively independent contractors, training usually either in small groups or alone, spread across the globe. There is no central authority responsible for providing injury updates, no team obligated to disclose an athlete's status, and few full-time reporters (shoutout to Jonathan Gault) dedicated to following individual competitors. So clearly, the infrastructure for in-depth reporting on every athlete at all times doesn’t exist in track. I’m not here to argue that this infrastructure *needs* to be built up. Actually, I think at times having so much reporting, like there is in the NBA, makes weeding through the fluff to find the real stories exceedingly difficult. I do think, however, athletes should share updates on their season if there are questions. Athletes *should feel obligated* to provide updates to their fans who invest their time, money, and emotions into them and the sport. I’m not asking for every athlete to turn into a social media personality, but is it so much to ask for periodic updates?

	Just like any other professional sport, track wants to be taken seriously. But how can people take it seriously when our best athletes can simply disappear for months, completely unaccounted for? The sport should feel lucky some fans are willing to dig through LetsRun.com threads for answers. But the more casual fan? A freshman in high school just being introduced to the sport? They move on, and they might not come back. Part of maintaining the sport’s following (and growing it) consists of treating the sport seriously, like any other major sport. 

To be fair, there's a reasonable case to be made on the other side. Athletes are not entertainers hired to perform for our amusement. They are human beings who happen to be very good at running. To that point, I would say I agree, but when you are inking professional shoe deals and earning prize money, I think you are signing yourself into an implicit contract with the sport and the fans whose attention makes those deals possible in the first place.

	And so, casting doping claims aside, we return to the simple, uncomfortable fact: elite athletes don't just vanish in the middle of seasons. With the circumstances still unknown, we can only hope Koech is healthy and doing well, and that whatever has kept him away from the sport is ultimately resolved. Until then, this track mystery remains just that.
items.author: Tate Sheehy
items.title: How to Disappear
